docs.title: Expert Mode
docs.text:
Designed for researchers and advanced users.

Displays:

docs.text:
Designed for APSRF experimentation.

The primary workflow is:

Upload Content ↓ Analyze ↓ Review Findings ↓ Review Recommendation ↓ Deploy

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The APSRF Core defines the foundational principles, architecture and operating philosophy of the Agentic Protocol Security Research Framework.

All APSRF components derive from the Core.

This includes:

  • Governance
  • Taxonomy
  • Metrics
  • Protocols
  • Experiments
  • Findings
  • Software Artifacts

The Core establishes the conceptual boundaries of the framework and provides the common language used throughout the ecosystem.

docs.title: What Is APSRF?
docs.text:
APSRF stands for:

Agentic Protocol Security Research Framework

APSRF is an experimental framework designed to study:

  • protocol behavior,
  • protocol governance,
  • contextual mediation,
  • protocol composition,
  • agent security,
  • observable AI behavior.

The framework operates entirely through documented and observable contextual layers.

APSRF does not require access to:

  • model weights,
  • hidden system prompts,
  • internal infrastructure,
  • platform internals.

Research is limited to observable outputs and documented protocol interactions.

docs.title: Primary Research Question
docs.text:
The original APSRF question was:

Can protocol ecosystems influence observable behavior through contextual architecture alone?

As the project evolved, additional questions emerged:

  • Can protocol ecosystems self-organize?
  • Can governance emerge from protocol structures?
  • Can protocol ecosystems generate software artifacts?
  • Can protocol ecosystems be used defensively?
  • Can protocol analysis become a security discipline?

docs.title: APSRF Philosophy
docs.text: The framework follows four principles.

docs.title: Observation First
docs.text:
Observations always take precedence over assumptions.

Claims must be supported by observable evidence.

docs.title: Reproducibility
docs.text:
Experiments should be documented and reproducible.

Procedures should be repeatable.

docs.title: Governance
docs.text:
Protocols require governance.

Uncontrolled protocol interaction can create instability.

docs.title: Separation of Observation and Interpretation
docs.text:
Observations describe what occurred.

Interpretations describe what the observation may imply.

The two must never be treated as equivalent.

docs.title: APSRF Architecture
docs.text:
The ecosystem evolved into the following conceptual architecture:

Governance ↓ APSRF Core ↓ BP1 Mediation Layer ↓ Taxonomy + Metrics ↓ Protocols ↓ Experiments ↓ Findings ↓ Software Artifacts

docs.title: Governance
docs.text:
Provides:

  • oversight,
  • bias control,
  • research boundaries,
  • protocol supervision.

Purpose:

Maintain ecosystem stability.

docs.title: BP1
docs.text:
The primary mediation layer.

Responsibilities:

  • Detection
  • Classification
  • Validation
  • Prioritization
  • Activation

Purpose:

Regulate protocol interaction.

docs.text:
Provides standardized protocol classification.

Purpose:

Create a common protocol language.

docs.title: Metrics
docs.text:

Purpose:

Measure protocol characteristics.

docs.title: Protocols
docs.text:
Operational units of behavior.

Purpose:

Implement ecosystem functionality.

docs.title: Experiments
docs.text:
Research activities used to evaluate APSRF concepts.

Purpose:

Generate evidence.

docs.title: Findings
docs.text:
Documented observations derived from experimentation.

Purpose:

Capture knowledge.

docs.title: APSRF Taxonomy
docs.text:
Protocols are categorized according to APSRF classification structures.

Examples include:

  • Functional
  • Structural
  • Communication
  • Research
  • Persistence
  • Behavioral
  • Governance
  • Meta
  • Experimental

Refer to:

Protocol Taxonomy Specification

for formal definitions.

docs.title: APSRF Metrics
docs.text:
The framework evaluates protocol behavior using multiple metrics.

Examples:

  • Consistency
  • Persistence
  • Transferability
  • Dominance
  • Adaptability
  • Amplification
  • Structural Influence
  • Behavioral Influence
  • Cognitive Influence
  • Emergence

Metric values use a 0–5 scale.

docs.title: Governance Model
docs.text:
APSRF governance exists to prevent:

  • uncontrolled protocol escalation,
  • instability,
  • unsupported conclusions,
  • protocol abuse,
  • experimental contamination.

Governance does not enforce truth.

Governance enforces methodological discipline.

docs.title: Experimental Methodology
docs.text:
Every APSRF experiment follows:

Hypothesis ↓ Protocol Definition ↓ Execution ↓ Observation ↓ Interpretation ↓ Finding ↓ Documentation

This structure creates traceability across the framework.

docs.text:
As APSRF matured, software artifacts emerged.

docs.text:
APSRF does not claim:

  • model modification,
  • infrastructure access,
  • hidden prompt access,
  • weight manipulation,
  • platform control.

The framework studies:

  • observable behavior,
  • contextual systems,
  • protocol ecosystems.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The APSRF Dashboard is the first software artifact generated from the APSRF ecosystem.

It was created as part of EXP-004 and represents the transition of APSRF from a documentation-based research framework into a software-based operational environment.

The dashboard serves as a visual representation of:

  • APSRF Core
  • BP1
  • Governance
  • Metrics
  • Taxonomy

  • Audit History

The dashboard is not intended to represent AI internals.

It represents APSRF concepts only.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The dashboard was designed to answer a simple question:

Can APSRF documentation generate a coherent software artifact?

The experiment focused on:

  • entity extraction,
  • architecture generation,
  • interface generation,
  • software implementation.

docs.title: Historical Context
docs.text:
Prior to the dashboard experiment APSRF existed only as:

  • markdown documents,
  • protocol specifications,
  • methodology files,
  • research reports.

The dashboard became the first attempt to externalize APSRF concepts into an operational interface.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Determine whether APSRF documentation contains enough structural information to generate software.

The complete APSRF ecosystem was provided.

The implementation process required:

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Experiments are the practical foundation of APSRF.

While the Core, Taxonomy, Metrics and Governance documents define the framework, experiments provide the observable evidence used to evaluate hypotheses, validate protocol behavior and identify emergent effects.

All APSRF findings originate from experimental observations.

docs.title: Experimental Philosophy
docs.text:
APSRF experiments are designed to study:

  • protocol interaction,
  • protocol mediation,
  • governance behavior,
  • conflict resolution,
  • emergent protocol effects,
  • contextual influence.

Experiments focus exclusively on observable behavior.

APSRF does not claim access to:

  • model weights,
  • hidden system prompts,
  • internal infrastructure,
  • platform internals.

Only observable outputs are evaluated.

docs.title: Experimental Lifecycle
docs.text:
Every APSRF experiment follows the same structure:

Hypothesis ↓ Protocol Definition ↓ Execution ↓ Observation ↓ Interpretation ↓ Finding ↓ Documentation

This process is defined in the APSRF Experimental Methodology.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Findings are the primary research outputs of APSRF.

A finding represents a documented observation that emerged during experimentation and was considered significant enough to justify independent documentation.

Findings are not assumptions.

Findings originate from:

  • experiments,
  • protocol interactions,
  • governance observations,
  • software generation exercises,
  • protocol composition studies.

Each finding should be reproducible through documented experimental procedures.

docs.title: Finding Lifecycle
docs.text:
Observation ↓ Replication ↓ Interpretation ↓ Documentation ↓ Finding

A finding becomes valid only after sufficient evidence has been collected.

docs.title: Behavioral Findings
docs.text:
Related to observable protocol behavior.

Examples:

  • protocol persistence,
  • protocol amplification,
  • protocol adaptation.

docs.title: Governance Findings
docs.text:
Related to mediation and governance structures.

Examples:

  • governance erosion,
  • validation bottlenecks,
  • authority escalation.

docs.title: Emergent Findings
docs.text:
Related to effects not explicitly defined in protocol specifications.

Examples:

  • convergence bias,
  • influence accumulation,
  • protocol dominance.

docs.title: Architectural Findings
docs.text:
Related to APSRF itself.

Examples:

  • dashboard generation,
  • protocol composition behavior,
  • software artifact generation.

docs.text: Contextual Coherence Enhancement Layer (CCEL)

docs.title: Observation
docs.text:
A protocol designed to improve coherence and stability introduced indirect influence accumulation effects.

The protocol did not:

  • modify governance,
  • modify validation,
  • modify protocol priorities.

However, repeated optimization toward coherence created a tendency toward dominance by previously successful protocol combinations.

APSRF

APSRF is an experimental research framework designed to study protocol governance, protocol security, contextual mediation and emergent behavior within AI agent ecosystems.

The framework investigates how structured protocol systems influence observable behavior through contextual architecture without requiring access to model weights, hidden instructions, platform internals or infrastructure components.

To create a structured and reproducible methodology for studying protocol-driven AI ecosystems, identifying protocol-level risks and developing governance mechanisms capable of supporting future agent environments.

APSRF does not investigate:

Research is limited to observable outputs and documented protocol ecosystems.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Protocols are the operational building blocks of APSRF.

They define:

  • behavior,
  • mediation,
  • governance,
  • communication,
  • classification,
  • interaction rules.

Protocols are the mechanism through which APSRF structures and evaluates contextual ecosystems.

Every protocol within APSRF belongs to a defined taxonomy and may participate in governance, execution, communication, auditing or mediation activities.

docs.title: What Is A Protocol?
docs.text:
Within APSRF, a protocol is a structured set of instructions designed to influence, organize or regulate behavior within a contextual ecosystem.

Protocols may:

  • organize information,
  • mediate interactions,
  • classify content,
  • establish governance,
  • evaluate risk,
  • coordinate execution.

Protocols do not require access to system internals.

They operate entirely within observable contextual layers.

docs.title: Protocol Lifecycle
docs.text: Creation ↓ Classification ↓ Validation ↓ Activation ↓ Observation ↓ Evaluation ↓ Documentation

docs.title: Protocol Categories
docs.text:
Protocols are classified using the APSRF Taxonomy.

Categories include:

  • Functional
  • Structural
  • Communication
  • Research
  • Persistence
  • Behavioral
  • Governance
  • Meta
  • Experimental

Refer to:

[[Core]]

for detailed taxonomy definitions.

docs.title: Protocol Relationships
docs.text:
Protocols may interact with other protocols.

Common relationships include:

docs.title: Dependency
docs.text:
A protocol requires another protocol to operate.

Example:

BP1 depends on protocol classification structures.

docs.title: Mediation
docs.text:
A protocol evaluates or regulates another protocol.

Example:

BP1 mediates protocol activation.

docs.title: Governance
docs.text:
A protocol influences ecosystem governance.

Example:

Governance Framework protocols.

docs.title: Communication
docs.text:
Protocols that improve information exchange.

Example:

CRL, HCEP, HRR.

docs.title: Protocol Governance
docs.text:
All protocols operate under APSRF Governance.

Protocols may be:

  • approved,
  • conditionally approved,
  • rejected,
  • archived.

Governance exists to preserve ecosystem stability and research integrity.

docs.title: Protocol Composition
docs.text:
Protocols may be combined.

Composition can create:

  • beneficial effects,
  • neutral effects,
  • emergent effects,
  • governance conflicts.

Protocol composition remains an active research area within APSRF.

docs.text:
